overview.summary: The SPACEHOP Activity group launches a multi-stage cyber attack against a water utility company. The attack begins with a phishing campaign targeting employees, followed by the deployment of a SCADA-specific backdoor. The attackers establish persistence, escalate privileges, disable security tools, and ultimately disrupt the water treatment process through data destruction.

stage.name: Initial Access
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 164 Name: Mobile Phishing Description: An adversary targets mobile phone users with a phishing attack for the purpose of soliciting account passwords or sensitive information from the user. Mobile Phishing is a variation of the Phishing social engineering technique where the attack is initiated via malicious links or messages.
stage.description: The attacker sends a phishing email to an employee working remotely, leveraging a malicious Excel file containing a macro that executes a PowerShell script upon opening.

stage.name: Execution
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 542 Name: Targeted Malware Description: An adversary develops targeted malware that takes advantage of a known vulnerability in an organizational information technology environment. The malware crafted for these attacks is based specifically on information gathered about the technology environment.
stage.description: The attacker drops a custom-built SCADA-specific backdoor onto the compromised system, allowing remote control of the water/wastewater systems' SCADA interface.

stage.name: Persistence
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 579 Name: Replace Winlogon Helper DLL Description: Winlogon is a part of Windows that performs logon actions. In Windows systems prior to Windows Vista, a registry key can be modified that causes Winlogon to load a DLL on startup. Adversaries may take advantage of this feature to load adversary-defined code during system boot.
stage.description: The attacker schedules the SCADA backdoor to run periodically using the built-in task scheduler, ensuring persistence across reboots.

stage.name: Privilege Escalation
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 121 Name: Exploit Non-Production Interfaces Description: An adversary exploits a sample, demonstration, test, or debug interface that is unintentionally enabled on a production system, with the goal of gaining information or leveraging functionality that would otherwise be unavailable.
stage.description: The attacker identifies and exploits a vulnerability in a third-party service used by the water utility, such as a water pump controller, to escalate privileges to SCADA system controls.

stage.name: Defense Evasion
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 578 Name: Disable Security Software Description: An adversary exploits a weakness in access control to disable security tools so that detection does not occur. This can take the form of killing processes, deleting registry keys so that tools do not start at runtime, deleting log files, or similar actions.
stage.description: The attacker disables or removes security logging from critical servers, preventing the detection of unauthorized access and activities.

stage.name: Impact
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 675 Name: Retrieve Data from Decommissioned Devices Description: An adversary obtains decommissioned, recycled, or discarded systems and devices that can include an organization's intellectual property, employee data, and other types of controlled information. Systems and devices that have been improperly sanitized may retain sensitive information.
stage.description: The attacker disrupts the operation of the water treatment process by overloading pumps or shutting down critical components, leading to potential environmental harm or service interruption.

Scenario.summary: The attack begins with a phishing campaign targeting employees, followed by the deployment of a SCADA-specific backdoor. The attackers establish persistence, escalate privileges, disable security tools, and ultimately disrupt the water treatment process through data destruction.
